title: DU-VPT: Decomposed Uncertainty-Guided Visual Prompt Tuning for Test-Time Adaptation
description: A test-time adaptation method that decomposes layer-wise uncertainty into aleatoric and epistemic components to diagnose distribution shift types, then applies targeted visual prompts only at uncertain layers. Unlike prior work that uses single uncertainty metrics, we identify whether shifts are low-level (corruption) or semantic (domain), matching prompt types to shift characteristics.
motivation: Current TTA methods either apply prompts uniformly across all layers or use single uncertainty metrics for layer selection. However, different distribution shifts affect different layers differently—noise impacts early layers while domain shifts affect deep layers. By decomposing uncertainty into aleatoric (data) and epistemic (model) components, we can diagnose shift types and apply targeted adaptation, outperforming uniform approaches while maintaining parameter efficiency.
proposed_approach: DU-VPT consists of: (1) Lightweight uncertainty decomposition at each layer using local feature consistency (aleatoric) and calibration set deviation (epistemic); (2) Layer-wise shift diagnosis that classifies shifts as low-level, semantic, or mixed based on uncertainty patterns; (3) Targeted prompt injection: structure-aware prompts for low-level shifts, semantic prompts for domain shifts; (4) Entropy minimization for prompt updates with frozen backbone.
key_contribution: Novel uncertainty decomposition for TTA that diagnoses shift types, enabling targeted prompt adaptation. Unlike PALM (weight updates, single uncertainty metric), we use prompts with decomposed uncertainty. Provides theoretical justification and empirical evidence for why selective prompt injection outperforms uniform application.

related_work.paper: Visual Prompt Tuning (Jia et al., ECCV 2022)
related_work.distinction: VPT uses fixed layer strategies (shallow-only or deep-only) at training time. We dynamically select layers at test time based on uncertainty decomposition and diagnose shift types.
E2VPT improves training-time prompt efficiency via expert aggregation. We focus on test-time adaptation with shift-type-aware prompt selection.

related_work.relationship: Related work in test-time prompt tuning. DynaPrompt selects prompts from a buffer (horizontal selection), while DU-VPT selects layers for prompt injection (vertical selection).
key_differences: DynaPrompt: Horizontal selection (which prompt from buffer)
DU-VPT: Vertical selection (which layer depth based on shift type)
DynaPrompt: Text prompts for CLIP; DU-VPT: Visual prompts for ViTs

related_work.relationship: Related but distinct. PALM uses single uncertainty metric (gradient magnitude) for layer selection and updates weights. We decompose uncertainty into aleatoric/epistemic to diagnose shift types and use prompts instead of weight updates.
key_differences: PALM: Single uncertainty metric; DU-VPT: Decomposed (aleatoric + epistemic)
PALM: Weight updates at selected layers; DU-VPT: Prompt injection (frozen backbone)
PALM: Selects layers by magnitude; DU-VPT: Diagnoses shift type from patterns

related_work.distinction: TPT adapts text prompts for vision-language models uniformly. We adapt visual prompts for ViTs with layer-selective injection based on uncertainty decomposition.
DePT uses hierarchical regularization for visual prompts but applies them uniformly. We use decomposed uncertainty to select both which layers and which prompt types to use.
Tent updates BN statistics uniformly. We target specific layers with prompts based on shift diagnosis, applicable to ViTs without BN.

related_work.relationship: Foundational work on uncertainty decomposition. We apply these concepts to test-time adaptation with a lightweight estimation method suitable for real-time inference.

transparency_statement: We acknowledge PALM as the closest related work in terms of layer-selective adaptation. However, DU-VPT makes distinct contributions: (1) uncertainty decomposition vs. single metric, (2) shift-type diagnosis, (3) prompts vs. weight updates, (4) theoretical analysis of why selective prompts work.
hypothesis: Decomposing layer-wise uncertainty into aleatoric and epistemic components enables accurate diagnosis of distribution shift types. Targeted prompt injection based on this diagnosis outperforms both uniform prompt application and single-metric layer selection with weight updates.

confirmed: Within 1% accuracy of weight-update adaptation (Ablation 1 - Prompt vs Weight)
Lower catastrophic forgetting than PALM (>5% gap)
Interpretable layer selection patterns revealing model behavior
refuted: Selective prompt injection provides <1% improvement over uniform
Decomposed uncertainty performs equivalently to single metrics
Prompt adaptation underperforms weight updates by >2%

key_differentiators: First to apply uncertainty decomposition (aleatoric/epistemic) to test-time adaptation
Shift-type diagnosis: distinguishes low-level corruption from semantic shift
Targeted prompt types matched to identified shifts
Comprehensive ablation comparing prompts vs weight updates at selected layers
Interpretable insights into layer-wise model behavior under distribution shift
venue_target: NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, CVPR, ICCV, ECCV
